title: Exploring Enterprise Linux
description: Enterprise Linux for developers and early-career professionals — from first SSH login to production system administration.

items.title: Linux Capabilities: Breaking Up Root
items.content_html: Root isn't one switch — it's around forty distinct privileges. Capabilities let you grant one without the other thirty-nine: the basis of least privilege and rootless containers.

items.title: Namespaces and cgroups: How Linux Isolates a Process
items.content_html: What the kernel uses to decide what a process can see and how much it can consume — namespaces for visibility, cgroups for limits, the primitives behind every container.

items.title: Arguments and Exit Codes
items.content_html: Handle script arguments and exit codes in Bash. Learn $1, $@, $#, $?, and how to return meaningful exit codes that other scripts and tools can act on.

items.title: Conditionals
items.content_html: Write Bash conditionals that work reliably. Covers if/elif/else, the [[ ]] operator, common file and string tests, and patterns that avoid subtle bugs.

items.title: Your First Bash Script
items.content_html: Write your first Bash script with confidence. Covers the shebang line, script structure, making scripts executable, and where to store them for team use.

items.title: Functions
items.content_html: Organise Bash scripts with functions. Learn to define, call, and scope functions with local variables, return values via exit codes, and reuse patterns.

items.title: Loops
items.content_html: Master Bash loops for automation. Covers for loops over lists and files, while loops with command output, break and continue, and practical loop patterns.

items.title: Overview
items.content_html: A six-article bash scripting series for IT professionals. From your first shebang line to reusable functions — variables, arguments, conditionals, loops.

items.title: Variables and Quoting
items.content_html: Master Bash variables and quoting rules. Learn when to use double vs single quotes, command substitution, and how quoting bugs silently break your scripts.

items.title: Overview
items.content_html: Orient yourself in the Linux command line. Four articles covering navigation, the filesystem structure, finding files, and getting help when you're stuck.

items.title: Overview
items.content_html: The Linux commands and concepts every sysadmin and platform engineer must own. Five categories from command line fundamentals to Bash scripting automation.

items.title: Overview
items.content_html: Every command you run spawns a process. This section starts with processes — how to see them, manage them, and understand what they're doing on your system.

items.title: Overview
items.content_html: Learn how Linux handles text. Two articles covering pipes, redirection, and grep — the tools you reach for every time you investigate a Linux system.

items.title: Overview
items.content_html: Master Linux's permission model. Two articles covering file permissions and user/group management — the foundation of every access control decision on Linux.

items.title: Processes
items.content_html: Understand Linux process management — ps, top, kill, signals, process states, and the tools every sysadmin uses to investigate and control a running system.

items.title: Users and Groups
items.content_html: Master Linux user and group management — useradd, usermod, groupadd, service accounts, and the /etc/passwd and /etc/group files every sysadmin reads.

items.title: Getting Access
items.content_html: Set up your first Linux environment via SSH or a local VM. Step-by-step guidance to get safely connected and ready to start exploring your new system.

items.title: Overview
items.content_html: Linux survival guide for developers — SSH, orientation, permissions, logs, documentation, and production safety rules. Get effective without becoming a sysadmin.

items.title: grep
items.content_html: Master grep for Linux — regular expressions, recursive search, context flags, and the real-world patterns every sysadmin uses for log analysis and system investigation.

items.title: Pipes and Redirection
items.content_html: Master Linux pipes and redirection — combine commands into powerful pipelines, redirect output to files, and manage stdin, stdout, and stderr like a pro.
